column2: An application must recover from lost data and handle messages that arrive out of order. Which transport protocol behavior fits this requirement?
column4: Use TCP because it provides connection-oriented, reliable delivery behavior.
column5:
Why

TCP handles transport overhead such as recovery and ordering so the application does not have to solve those problems itself.

Exam Trap

Do not choose UDP when the requirement specifically calls for retransmission or ordering at the transport layer.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.6; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 2.1
column10: A+::220-1201::2.1 A+::220-1201::Domain2-Networking A+::220-1201::IPAddressingandCommonPorts Basic HighYield Scenario TCP Transport Source::Messer-v170

column2: A real-time service can tolerate some missing data but cannot wait for retransmissions. Which transport protocol is the better fit?
column5:
Why

UDP is connectionless and does not provide TCP-style recovery, ordering, or flow control. That tradeoff can fit time-sensitive traffic.

Exam Trap

UDP is not “better” than TCP. It is better only when the scenario values low-delay connectionless delivery over transport-layer recovery.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.6; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 2.1
column10: A+::220-1201::2.1 A+::220-1201::Domain2-Networking A+::220-1201::IPAddressingandCommonPorts Basic HighYield Scenario UDP Transport Source::Messer-v170

column2: A firewall rule allows TCP 53, but DNS lookups from clients still fail. What detail should you verify in the rule?
column4: Verify that the rule allows the expected protocol and port, such as UDP 53 for standard DNS queries in this objective.
column5:
Why

Port numbers are not enough by themselves. TCP and UDP have separate port spaces, so the transport protocol matters.

Real Help Desk

When a rule “opens the port” but traffic still fails, check whether it opened the correct transport protocol.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.6; Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.7; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 2.1
column10: A+::220-1201::2.1 A+::220-1201::Domain2-Networking A+::220-1201::IPAddressingandCommonPorts Basic HighYield Scenario Firewall Ports Source::Messer-v170

column2: A client connects to a web service. The server listens on a well-known service port, while the client uses a temporary port chosen for that session. What is the temporary client-side port called?
column5:
Why

Servers commonly listen on stable service ports. Clients commonly use temporary ports for individual conversations.

Exam Trap

Do not assume both sides of a conversation use the same port number.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.6; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 2.1
column10: A+::220-1201::2.1 A+::220-1201::Domain2-Networking A+::220-1201::IPAddressingandCommonPorts Basic Scenario Ports Sockets Source::Messer-v170

column2: A technician needs remote terminal access to a network device, but credentials should not be sent in clear text. Which protocol should replace Telnet?
column5:
Why

SSH provides an encrypted remote communication link. Telnet provides remote console-style access but sends communication in clear text.

Exam Trap

Keep the security distinction separate from the port numbers: SSH uses TCP 22, and Telnet uses TCP 23.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.7; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 2.1
column10: A+::220-1201::2.1 A+::220-1201::Domain2-Networking A+::220-1201::IPAddressingandCommonPorts Basic HighYield Scenario SSH Telnet Source::Messer-v170

column2: A new workstation should automatically receive an IP address, subnet mask, and other network options. Which service provides that configuration?
column5:
Why

DHCP provides automatic IP configuration from a server, appliance, or integrated network device such as a SOHO router.

Related Cards

2.1-C010 tests the DHCP UDP port association.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.7; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 2.1
column10: A+::220-1201::2.1 A+::220-1201::Domain2-Networking A+::220-1201::IPAddressingandCommonPorts Basic HighYield Scenario DHCP IPConfiguration Source::Messer-v170

column2: A user can reach a server by IP address but not by its hostname. Which service path should you check first?
column5:
Why

DNS converts names to IP addresses. If direct IP access works but names fail, name resolution is the focused path to investigate.

Exam Trap

Do not start by replacing the network adapter when the symptom points to name resolution.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.7; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 2.1
column10: A+::220-1201::2.1 A+::220-1201::Domain2-Networking A+::220-1201::IPAddressingandCommonPorts Basic HighYield Scenario DNS Troubleshooting Source::Messer-v170

column2: An email client can receive messages but cannot send outgoing mail. Which mail protocol path should you check for sending?
column5:
Why

SMTP is associated with mail transfer and sending mail from a device to a mail server. POP3 and IMAP are used for receiving mail.

Exam Trap

Do not use POP3 or IMAP as the sending protocol just because the problem involves email.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.7; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 2.1
column10: A+::220-1201::2.1 A+::220-1201::Domain2-Networking A+::220-1201::IPAddressingandCommonPorts Basic Scenario Email SMTP Source::Messer-v170

column2: A user can browse websites and receive email, but cannot reach a shared Windows folder or printer. Which service protocol should be investigated for file and printer sharing?
column5:
Why

SMB is the Windows file-sharing and printer-sharing protocol family. This card tests service recognition, not the exact port recall covered by 2.1-C013.

Exam Trap

Do not troubleshoot this as a web, mail, or DNS issue when the symptom is specifically Windows file/printer sharing.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.7; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 2.1
